title: Commission and covenant charge
summary_text: After Moses dies, the LORD commissions Joshua to lead Israel across the Jordan and promises his presence, success, and courage if Joshua keeps the law before him. Joshua orders the tribes to prepare for battle, and the eastern tribes pledge to help their brothers until the land is won. The chapter establishes that the conquest will depend on obedience to God's word and confidence in God's presence.
key_points: Joshua is told to rise and lead the people into the land.
Success is tied to meditating on and obeying the law.
The eastern tribes promise to fight alongside Israel.
God commands courage, not fear.
The people answer Joshua with loyal support.
evidence_phrases: Moses my servant is dead
be strong and courageous
the book of the law shall not depart
the LORD your God is with you
all that you command us we will do

title: Rahab and the spies
summary_text: Joshua sends two spies into Jericho, where Rahab hides them and confesses that the LORD has given Israel the land. She bargains for mercy for her family, and the spies swear to spare everyone gathered inside her house. Their safe return confirms that fear has fallen on the land and that Rahab's faith has already joined her to Israel's hope.
key_points: Rahab hides the spies from the king's men.
She confesses the LORD's supremacy over Canaan.
The spies promise safety to her household.
The scarlet cord becomes the sign of rescue.
The spies report that the land's courage has melted.
evidence_phrases: I know that the LORD has given you the land
she hid them with the stalks of flax
the LORD your God, he is God
the line of scarlet thread
our hearts did melt

title: Crossing the Jordan
summary_text: As the ark moves ahead of the people, Israel follows the priests toward the swollen Jordan River and waits for God's signal. When the priests set their feet in the water, the river stops flowing and the nation crosses on dry ground. The miracle confirms that the living God is among them and that Joshua is exalted before the people as Moses once was.
key_points: The ark goes before the people into the river.
The priests stand in the Jordan until all pass over.
The waters are cut off from upstream.
Joshua is magnified in the sight of Israel.
The crossing marks a new beginning in the land.
evidence_phrases: the ark of the covenant of the Lord
the priests bearing the ark
the waters which came down from above stood
on dry ground
the living God is among you

title: Memorial stones at Gilgal
summary_text: Twelve men take twelve stones from the Jordan as a memorial while twelve stones are set up where the priests stood. Joshua explains that future generations will ask about the stones, and parents will tell them how the LORD cut off the Jordan before Israel. The memorial turns the crossing into an enduring testimony about the power of God and the obedience of his people.
key_points: Stones are taken from the riverbed and set at Gilgal.
The memorial is meant to teach future children.
Joshua sets up a second memorial in the Jordan.
The LORD made Israel cross on dry ground.
The crossing echoes the Red Sea deliverance.
evidence_phrases: what do these stones mean
that this may be a sign among you
the LORD cut off the waters of the Jordan
these stones shall be for a memorial
that all the peoples of the earth may know

title: Circumcision, Passover, and the commander
summary_text: Israel is circumcised at Gilgal, the reproach of Egypt is rolled away, and the people keep the Passover in the land for the first time. The manna ceases once they eat the produce of Canaan, signaling that the wilderness era is over. Joshua then meets the commander of the LORD's army, who demands reverence and reminds him that the campaign belongs to God.
key_people: the commander of the LORD's army
key_points: The new generation is circumcised at Gilgal.
The Passover is kept after entering the land.
The manna stops when they eat the produce of Canaan.
Joshua removes his sandals in reverence.
The commander identifies the campaign as the LORD's.
evidence_phrases: roll away the reproach of Egypt
keep the Passover on the fourteenth day
the manna ceased on the next day
remove your sandals from off your foot
the place where you stand is holy

title: Jericho falls
summary_text: The LORD gives Joshua an unusual battle plan for Jericho: march, blow trumpets, shout, and let God bring the walls down. After seven days the city collapses, but Rahab and her family are spared because she believed and hid the spies. Joshua places a curse on the ruined city, showing that the victory is the LORD's and not Israel's strategy.
key_points: Jericho falls by obedience, not siege engines.
The walls collapse after the shout of faith.
Rahab and her household are rescued.
The city is devoted to destruction.
Joshua pronounces a curse on rebuilding Jericho.
evidence_phrases: the city shall be accursed
the wall fell down flat
only Rahab the prostitute shall live
the LORD has given you the city
the people shouted with a great shout

title: Achan's hidden sin
summary_text: Israel's confidence at Ai turns to shame when the army flees and thirty-six men die because Achan kept what was devoted. Joshua seeks the LORD, and the answer exposes hidden covenant breach as the true cause of defeat. After the lot falls on Achan, Israel stones him and his household, removing the trouble from among them so the conquest can continue.
key_points: Israel loses at Ai because someone kept devoted things.
Joshua laments the defeat and seeks God's answer.
The lot identifies Achan as the offender.
Achan confesses his theft from the spoil of Jericho.
Judgment removes the sin that was troubling Israel.
evidence_phrases: Israel has sinned
the anger of the LORD burned
I saw among the spoil
when I saw, I coveted
why have you troubled us

title: Ai defeated and the law read
summary_text: Joshua returns to Ai with a planned ambush, and the city is captured after its king is drawn out and destroyed. The chapter then shifts to covenant worship as Joshua builds an altar on Mount Ebal and reads the law to all Israel. Victory is joined to obedience, showing that conquest and covenant must remain together.
key_points: Ai is taken by ambush after the first defeat.
Joshua burns the city and its king.
An altar is built on Mount Ebal.
The law is written and read in full before the people.
Israel hears both blessing and curse.
evidence_phrases: lay wait against the city
the king of Ai he hanged
an altar of whole stones
he read all the words of the law
there was not a word of all that Moses commanded

title: The Gibeonite deception
summary_text: The Gibeonites disguise themselves as travelers and trick Israel into making a covenant without asking counsel from the LORD. When the deception is exposed, Joshua honors the oath but makes them servants in the sanctuary service. Their story warns that appearances can mislead even when the covenant must still be kept.
key_points: Israel is deceived by worn-out supplies and false claims.
The leaders swear peace without asking the LORD.
The covenant cannot be broken after it is sworn.
The Gibeonites become servants for the sanctuary.
The lesson is to seek God's counsel first.
evidence_phrases: asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD
from a very far country
we are your servants
Joshua made peace with them
hewers of wood and drawers of water

title: Sun and moon stand still
summary_text: When the five kings of the south attack Gibeon, Joshua marches all night to rescue the city and the LORD throws the enemy into confusion. Hailstones strike the fleeing army, and Joshua asks for the sun and moon to stand still until the victory is complete. The kings are trapped, executed, and buried, proving that the LORD fights for Israel in heaven and on earth.
key_points: Joshua rescues Gibeon by forced march.
The LORD sends confusion and hailstones.
Joshua prays for extended daylight.
The sun and moon stand still until the battle ends.
The defeated kings are publicly executed.
evidence_phrases: the LORD threw them into confusion
the sun stood still
the moon stayed
there was no day like that before it
the LORD fought for Israel

title: Northern kings defeated
summary_text: A final northern coalition gathers under Jabin of Hazor, but Joshua strikes them quickly and leaves no rival army standing. Horses are hamstrung, chariots burned, and Hazor destroyed as the LORD gives the land into Israel's hand. The chapter emphasizes total victory through steady obedience rather than panic or delay.
key_points: The northern kings unite against Israel.
Joshua attacks before they can regroup.
Horses are hamstrung and chariots burned.
Hazor is singled out for destruction.
The land finally rests from war.
evidence_phrases: as many as the sand that is on the seashore
you shall hamstring their horses
burn their chariots with fire
Joshua took all that land
the land had rest from war

title: Conquered kings summarized
summary_text: Joshua lists the kings defeated east of the Jordan under Moses and west of the Jordan under Joshua. The inventory reads like a conquest ledger, reminding Israel that every victory came by the LORD's promise rather than by human power. By naming the kings, the chapter turns past battles into public testimony.
key_points: The chapter catalogs kings east of the Jordan.
It then lists kings defeated by Joshua.
The record connects conquest to promise.
The tally confirms broad, settled victory.
The list reinforces Israel's memory of God's acts.
evidence_phrases: these are the kings of the land
whom Moses struck
whom Joshua and the children of Israel struck
one king
all together thirty-one kings

title: Land still remaining
summary_text: Joshua is old, but the LORD tells him there is still much land to possess, so the chapter turns to the inheritance already given east of the Jordan. The tribes of Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh receive their inheritance, while Levi receives no land portion because the LORD himself is their inheritance. The chapter marks a transition from conquest to settlement and distribution.
key_points: Joshua is told that much land still remains.
East Jordan inheritance is listed first.
The Levites receive the LORD as their portion.
The chapter shifts from conquest to allotment.
God's promise is larger than what has yet been occupied.
evidence_phrases: there remains yet very much land to be possessed
the tribes beyond Jordan eastward
the LORD, the God of Israel, is their inheritance
Moses gave no inheritance to the tribe of Levi
their cities and their villages

title: Caleb receives Hebron
summary_text: Caleb reminds Joshua of the promise made through Moses and asks for the hill country at Hebron, still occupied by the Anakim. Joshua blesses him, and Caleb drives out the giants by faith in the LORD. The chapter highlights steadfast believing after decades of waiting and rewards that faith with inheritance.
key_points: Caleb asks for the mountain promised to him.
Joshua blesses him and gives Hebron.
Caleb relies on the LORD's help against giants.
Faithful endurance is rewarded after many years.
The chapter shows promise fulfilled in old age.
evidence_phrases: as the LORD spoke
wholly followed the LORD my God
give me this mountain
the children of Anak were there
I shall be able to drive them out

title: Judah's inheritance
summary_text: The boundaries and towns of Judah are laid out in detail, showing the size and centrality of the tribe's inheritance in the south. Caleb's daughter Achsah asks for springs, and Caleb gives her the upper and lower springs in addition to the land. The chapter closes with a concrete picture of promise becoming geography, settlement, and provision.
key_points: Judah's territory is measured and assigned.
Achsah asks for springs to match her land.
Caleb grants both the upper and lower springs.
The chapter records towns, borders, and settlements.
Inheritance includes both land and practical provision.
evidence_phrases: this then is the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah
give me a blessing
give me also springs of water
the upper springs and the lower springs
he gave her the upper springs and the lower springs

title: Ephraim's inheritance
summary_text: The descendants of Joseph receive their boundaries, but the chapter notes that the Canaanites continue to live in Gezer among Ephraim. The allotment shows promise fulfilled and incomplete obedience side by side. Israel has land, yet the conquest is not finished in every city.
key_points: Ephraim's borders are described in detail.
The Canaanites remain in Gezer.
The chapter shows partial possession of the land.
Joseph's line receives a clear inheritance.
Some enemies are still present within the territory.
evidence_phrases: the lot for the children of Joseph
the border went out
the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites
they did not drive them out
Gezer

title: Manasseh's inheritance
summary_text: The half-tribe of Manasseh receives its territory, but the daughters of Zelophehad and the sons of Joseph both press Joshua for more room. Joshua tells them to clear the hill country by driving out the Canaanites, even if they must cut down the forest and trust in strength. The chapter balances rightful inheritance with the hard work still ahead.
key_points: Manasseh receives a broad but contested inheritance.
The daughters of Zelophehad confirm their claim.
Joseph's descendants ask for more space.
Joshua tells them to clear the forested hill country.
Possession still requires courage and effort.
evidence_phrases: we are a great people
go up to the forest
clear a place for yourself
you shall not have one lot only
the hill country

title: The remaining land surveyed
summary_text: Seven tribes delay taking their inheritance, so Joshua sends men to survey the remaining land and divide it into lots. Benjamin receives its portion, and the whole process is anchored at Shiloh where the tabernacle stands. The chapter shows that the land is now measured not for conquest but for ordered settlement under God's presence.
key_people: the men who survey the land
key_points: Seven tribes have not yet claimed their land.
Joshua orders a survey of the remaining territory.
The tabernacle at Shiloh anchors the allotment.
Benjamin's borders are described first.
The land is divided by lot under God's authority.
evidence_phrases: how long are you slack
describe the land in seven portions
the tent of meeting was there
the lot of the children of Benjamin
Joshua cast lots for them in Shiloh

title: The remaining tribal lots
summary_text: The final tribes receive their inheritance one by one, with Simeon, Zebulun, Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan all assigned territory. Dan's inheritance proves difficult enough that later conflict will push them elsewhere, and Joshua finally receives his own city. The chapter closes the land division process and gives the leader a modest portion among his people.
key_points: The remaining tribes receive their lots.
Joshua is given Timnath-serah in the hill country.
The chapter completes the tribal division process.
Dan's inheritance foreshadows later struggle.
Leadership ends with a simple personal allotment.
evidence_phrases: the lot came out
this is the inheritance
Joshua gave an inheritance among them
Timnath-serah
by the commandment of the LORD

title: Cities of refuge
summary_text: The LORD directs Joshua to appoint cities of refuge so that accidental killers can flee from vengeance and receive a fair hearing. Six cities are designated across the land, giving mercy and justice a visible home within Israel. The chapter shows that holiness includes protection for the innocent as well as judgment for the guilty.
key_points: Six refuge cities are appointed.
They protect accidental killers from vengeance.
Each case receives a hearing before the congregation.
The law distinguishes intent from accident.
Mercy is built into Israel's civic life.
evidence_phrases: cities of refuge
that the manslayer might flee there
until he stands before the congregation
without intent
for the children of Israel

title: Levitical cities and rest
summary_text: The Levites receive cities and pasture lands from every tribe, fulfilling the promises that God had spoken through Moses. The chapter repeatedly notes that not one good word failed and that the land finally had rest from war. It is a quiet but triumphant picture of covenant faithfulness becoming ordinary life.
key_points: The Levites are given cities among the tribes.
Their provision comes from Israel's inheritance.
Every promise from the LORD is fulfilled.
The land rests from war.
Priestly service is woven into settled life.
evidence_phrases: the LORD gave to Israel all the land
not one thing has failed
the land had rest from war
all came to pass
the LORD delivered all their enemies

title: The altar by the Jordan
summary_text: The eastern tribes build a large altar near the Jordan, and the western tribes fear that Israel has set up a rival place of worship. Phinehas and the leaders investigate, and the eastern tribes explain that the altar is only a witness between them and the rest of Israel. The crisis ends in peace, showing how zeal and explanation can prevent civil war.
key_points: An altar sparks fear of rebellion.
The western tribes prepare for war.
Phinehas investigates before fighting.
The eastern tribes explain the altar's purpose.
Peace is restored when motives are made clear.
evidence_phrases: a witness between us and you
what trespass is this
the congregation of the LORD
the mighty one, God, the LORD
it is a witness

title: Joshua's farewell warning
summary_text: Joshua, now old, gathers Israel and reminds them that the LORD has fought for them and driven out great nations before them. He warns that covenant faithfulness requires clinging to the LORD alone and avoiding the remaining peoples and their idols. The farewell turns the memory of victory into a charge for long-term obedience.
key_points: Joshua recalls God's victories over the nations.
He warns Israel not to mix with the survivors.
Idolatry is treated as a covenant threat.
Love and loyalty must be exclusive to the LORD.
The people are urged to keep every good promise.
evidence_phrases: be very courageous to keep and to do all that is written
cleave to the LORD your God
there shall not any one be able to stand before you
if you do at all go back
a snare and a trap

title: Covenant at Shechem and Joshua's death
summary_text: Joshua gathers the tribes at Shechem, recounts God's long history of deliverance from Abraham to the conquest and calling the people to choose whom they will serve. Israel renews the covenant, and Joshua sets up a witness stone before he dies, while Joseph's bones are buried and Eleazar also passes. The book ends by tying promise, response, and fulfillment together in one final act of remembrance.
key_points: Joshua rehearses Israel's history before the people.
The people are told to choose whom they will serve.
A covenant is renewed at Shechem.
Joshua sets up a witness stone under the oak.
Joseph's bones are buried and Joshua and Eleazar die.
evidence_phrases: choose you this day whom you will serve
as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD
the LORD our God he it is that brought us up
a stone for a witness
Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua
